State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5662        322 5660           322 5901           322 6441           322 6444

May 23, 1979

Joseph H. Schieffer
Zimmerman and Kalkstein
World Savings Tower
1970 Broadway, Suite 830
Oakland, CA  94612

Dear Mr. Schieffer:

Following up on today's telephone conversation, I am
returning the original and copy of the Statement of Organization for the Amalgamated Transit Union Local #1575 Political
Fund ("Union") and the Union's recipient committee campaign
statement covering the period from January 1 - December 31,
1978.  As we discussed, the Statement of Organization should
reflect that the Union received $500 in contributions and
qualified as a recipient committee on December 19, 1977, not
January 1, 1978.

On the campaign statement, the office being sought by
the candidate or of the officeholder should be listed.
Copies of the campaign statement should be filed in the
appropriate places, as indicated in Government Code Section
81005 and on pages 8 and 9 of the 1979 Information Manual on
Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act.

If you have any questions, please feel free to call.

Sincerely,

Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

BC:plh
Enclosures

LAW OFFICES OF

ZIMMERMAN AND KALKSTEIN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

WORLD SAVINGS TOWER • SUITE 830, 1970 BROADWAY • OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612 • (415) 465-2848

MICHAEL H. KALKSTEIN
ALAN LEE ZIMMERMAN
DONNA M.VENERUSO

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
MILLS BUILDING, SUITE 440
220 MONTGOMERY STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104

OF COUNSEL
WILLIAM E. TAGGART, JR.
THOMAS E. COOPER

REPLY TO: Oakland

May 22, 1979

Ms. Barbara Campbell
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Amalgamated Transit Union, Local 1575

Dear Ms. Campbell:

Pursuant to the advice which the Fair Political
Practices Commission issued under your signature on
April 18, 1979, enclosed please find forms completed
by the Amalgamated Transit Union, Local 1575 Political
Fund.

The enclosed forms include the original and
copy of a Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee)
and an original Recipient Committee Campaign Statement
with a continuation sheet, Schedule A, and two Schedule
E's. The time period of the enclosed statements commences with the institution of the Political Fund on
November 28, 1977 and terminates as of December 31,
1978. The Political Fund will continue to file the
necessary reporting forms pursuant to the schedule
stated in the Fair Political Practices Commission 1979
Information Manual on campaign disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act.

As you are aware, the Political Fund was
originally of the opinion that it was an expenditure
committee or a major donor committee but that it was
not a recipient committee. Only after receipt of your
opinion, was the Political Fund aware of the necessity

Barbara Campbell
May 22, 1979
Page 2


of additionally filing as a recipient committee due to
the adoption by the FPPC of the California Administrative
Code provisions relating to multi-purpose committees and
"earmarking".

     Once the FPPC advice was received by this office
immediate action was taken to complete and file the
enclosed forms.  Based on the good faith shown by the
Political Fund in seeking to determine its applicable
filing status under the Political Reform Act, there would
appear to be no reason for the imposition of any sanctions
against the Political Fund due to any delay in filing
the enclosed statements.

     If you have any questions regarding the information
contained in the enclosed statements, please contact me.

     Thank you very much for your cooperation.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Joseph H. Schieffer

JHS/jmm

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660           322-5901           322-6441           322-6444

April 18, 1979


Joseph H. Schieffer
Zimmerman and Kalkstein
World Savings Tower
1970 Broadway, Suite 830
Oakland, CA  94612

Dear Mr. Schieffer:

     This is in response to your request for advice under
Government Code Section 83114(b)1/ concerning the campaign
disclosure reporting requirements of the Amalgamated Transit
Union, Local 1575.

     The facts as I understand them are as follows:  Amalga-
mated Transit Union, Local 1575 ("Local"), will ask its
general membership to approve a resolution under which five
percent of the Local's regular dues income would be allocated
and used for political purposes.  Members pay dues to the
Local on a monthly basis.  The Local's Executive Board will
make decisions as to the candidates or committees, if any,
that will receive contributions.  If the Executive Board
decides not to make any contributions, the funds would be
reallocated to the general fund.

     You seek clarification on whether the Local will qualify
as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a).

     Section 82013(a) defines the type of committee that
receives contributions totaling $500 or more in a calendar
year.  There are two different types of Section 82013(a)
committees ("recipient committees").  The first type is a
primary purpose committee which is formed or existing pri-
marily for "political purposes."  The second type is a multi-
purpose committee which exists for a variety of purposes,


1/  All statutory references are to the Government
Code.

Joseph H. Schieffer
April 18, 1979
Page Two


one of which is to make contributions or expenditures in
connection with elections. From the facts you presented, it
appears the Local will be a multi-purpose committee since
its major activity is not making campaign contributions or
expenditures. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(c) and
(d), a copy of which is enclosed.

Payments received by a multi-purpose committee will be
contributions if the donor knows or has reason to know some
or all of the payment will be used to make contributions or
expenditures relative to elections. Therefore payments
received are reportable contributions if the payments are
"earmarked" for political purposes. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215(d).

If the resolution applies to dues already collected,
the members will not have earmarked those dues. Accordingly,
in the situation where the Local uses money previously collected
but not consisting of contributions received, the Local will
become a committee under Section 82013(c) when it makes
contributions or expenditures of $5,000 or more for political
purposes in a calendar year to or at the behest of other
committees or candidates and required to file campaign statements.

To the extent that the resolution applies to prospective
dues, the members will be specifically notified that five
percent of their dues will go for contributions or expenditures.
Therefore five percent of the dues received is earmarked
for political purposes and, as such, is a contribution.
Once $500 in contributions has been received, the Local has
qualified as a committee under Section 82013(a) and must
file, within ten days, a Statement of Organization with the
Secretary of State. If the Local had previously qualified
as a committee under Section 82013(c), its qualification as
a recipient committee will not change the requirement to
file campaign statements at the appropriate times. However,
once the Local qualifies as a recipient committee, it will
have to report contributions received and will be required
to itemize any person who contributes a pro rata share of
$100 or more or whose cumulative amount of contributions
totals $100 or more. From our telephone discussion, it

_____________________________

2/ To determine what portion of the payment will be a
contribution, the payment may be apportioned on any reasonable
basis. In this case, five percent is the most reasonable
basis.

Joseph H. Schieffer
April 18, 1979
Page Three


appears that the five percent standard will be applied to
the forthcoming monthly dues. The Local will then qualify
as a committee under Section 82013(a) when it receives $500
in contributions.

     I hope this information is helpful to you. If you have
any additional questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara Campbell
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

BC:plh
Enclosure

LAW OFFICES OF

ZIMMERMAN AND KALKSTEIN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

WORLD SAVINGS TOWER • SUITE 830, 1970 BROADWAY • OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612 • (415) 465-2848

MICHAEL H. KALKSTEIN
ALAN LEE ZIMMERMAN
DONNA M. VENERUSO

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
MILLS BUILDING, SUITE 440
220 MONTGOMERY STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104

OF COUNSEL
WILLIAM E. TAGGART, JR.
THOMAS E. COOPER

REPLY TO:   Oakland

April 10, 1979

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

RE:  Request for:  commission advice
     Requesting Party: Amalgamated Transit Union AFL-CIO,
     Local Division 1575

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

     This is a request for commission advice pursuant to
California Government Code, section 83114(b) and Title
2 of the California Administrative Code, section 18329.

     The requesting party hereby requests the Fair Political
Practices Commission to verify that if the requesting party
engaged in the course of conduct stated herein that said
requesting party would not be considered a recipient committee
within the meaning of Government Code section 82013(a).

FACTS

     The requesting party, Amalgamated Transit Union, Local
1575 (hereinafter "Local"), intends to engage in the following
activity: The general membership of the Local would approve
a resolution whereby five percent (5%) of the Local's regular
dues income (up to a maximum of $2,000.00 at any one time)
would be allocated and set aside for political purposes.
The funds so collected would be contributed to or at the
behest of candidates or committees with respect to candidates
for State or local office or ballot measures. The Executive
Board of the Local would decide which candidates or committees,
if any, would receive the contributions. If the Executive
Board of the Local, in its discretion, decided not to make
any contributions, the funds would be reallocated to the
Local general fund.

     The Local seeks confirmation that under this course
of conduct that, to the extent that it makes "independent
expenditures" totaling $500.00 or more in a calendar year

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
April 10, 1979
Page 2


or "contributions made to or at the behest of candidates
and committees" totaling $5,000.00 or more in a calendar
year, it is either an expenditure committee or a major
donor committee respectively; but that it is not a recipient
committee.

     This office has examined the proposal of the Local
and is of the opinion that under the plan outlined above
that the Local would not be a recipient committee. However,
due to the civil and criminal penalties which could result
if the Local was determined to be a recipient committee
and failed to file reports as such, the Local requests
that the opinion of this office be confirmed in a commission
advice issued by the FPPC.

     Our opinion that the Local is not a recipient committee
is based on the fact that no additional dues are raised
from members of the Local to finance the expenditures which
are made. Rather, there is merely an internal budgetary
decision made by the Executive Board whereby a portion
of the Local's assets can be allocated for political purposes.
It is contemplated that funds originally allocated for
political purposes which are not so spent will be realllocated
to the general fund.

     This office has already advised the Local that it
must file the reports required of those making independent
expenditures or major contributions to the extent that
the dollar minimums specified in Government Code sections
82013 (b) and (c) are met.

     If any further information is desired please contact
the undersigned.

     Thank you very much for your cooperation.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        JOSEPH H. SCHIEFFER

JHS:bt

sections.question_synthetic: Is the Amalgamated Transit Union, Local 1575 a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act if it allocates 5% of regular dues income (up to $2,000 at a time) for political contributions to candidates and committees?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request for advice letter dated April 10, 1979. The document also includes a follow-up correspondence from May 22, 1979 referencing FPPC advice issued April 18, 1979 on this same matter. Only the incoming request section was analyzed as primary document. Extraction confidence is good but not perfect due to some text truncation/OCR issues in the original document.

embedding.summary: An attorney requests FPPC advice on behalf of Amalgamated Transit Union Local 1575 regarding whether the union would be classified as a recipient committee if it allocates a portion of regular dues for political contributions to candidates and committees. The attorney argues the union should not be classified as a recipient committee because funds are reallocated from the general budget rather than raised through additional dues.
